network.overall_strategy: The network follows the literature consensus that prebiotic chemistry robustly generates racemic alanine through multiple convergent routes centered on HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and alpha-aminopropionitrile, while L-alanine is not produced by any single established abiotic reaction. Accordingly, the network is designed as a connected bipartite graph in which hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon and keto-acid hubs, surface chemistry supplies HCN-rich Strecker, cyanosulfidic, spark-discharge, and oligomer routes to DL-alanine, and a final biochemical/prebiotic systems-level symmetry-breaking module converts a DL-alanine pool into L-enriched alanine in a compartmentalized, network-level selection step. This final step is explicitly marked as speculative but grounded in the literature conclusion that terrestrial homochirality likely emerged through indirect amplification rather than direct enantioselective alanine synthesis.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 to a one-carbon reduced intermediate yielding formate.
reactions.reasoning: Formate production from CO2 and H2 on Fe-S mineral surfaces is central to alkaline hydrothermal vent models and provides reduced C1 feedstock for downstream carbonyl and keto-acid chemistry (Russell and Hall, 1997; Lane and Martin, 2012; Herschy et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2/H2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: vent-associated Fe/Ni mineral chemistry; reductive carboxylation network

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-promoted carbon fixation and C-C bond formation within an rTCA-like hydrothermal network generate pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a key prebiotic branch-point and has been generated in Fe-promoted reverse TCA analog chemistry and from CO2 reduction over Fe/Ni systems, justifying its placement as a hydrothermal hub (Muchowska, Varma, and Moran, 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde formation from CO2 and H2

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent analogue, pH gradient across mineral barriers

reactions.mechanism: Surface-mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 produces formaldehyde as a reduced carbonyl intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde production from H2 and CO2 under vent-like Fe(Ni)S catalysis was demonstrated in hydrothermal reactor systems and is relevant as an upstream carbonyl source for surface aldehyde chemistry (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Surface photoredox homologation to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pools; aldehyde-stabilizing evaporitic chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical homologation within HCN-derived reductive networks converts lower carbonyl feedstocks into acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland established photoredox access to aldehyde precursors needed for cyanosulfidic chemistry. This node is included as a connecting literature-grounded surface route from reduced C1 chemistry to acetaldehyde, while acknowledging that aldehyde networks are multistep rather than single-step in reality (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Classic Miller-Urey spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral collection and concentration on TiO2/clay shore surfaces
conditions.other: electrical discharge atmosphere above aqueous reservoir; one-week spark regime

reactions.mechanism: Spark discharge through reducing gas mixtures generates nitriles and carbonyl compounds that dissolve into the aqueous phase.
reactions.reasoning: Miller's original experiments generated amino acid precursors including HCN and aldehydes in situ, with alanine later produced through Strecker chemistry in the water phase. Mineral agents here represent post-discharge surface concentration rather than the electrical source itself, satisfying the network mineral requirement without changing the literature chemistry (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S spark generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral collection and concentration on irradiated surfaces
conditions.other: spark discharge in H2S-containing volcanic atmosphere; enhanced precursor productivity

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in sulfur-bearing atmosphere produces nitriles and aldehydes with enhanced amino-acid precursor distributions.
reactions.reasoning: H2S-containing Miller-type discharge experiments yield substantially enhanced alanine-relevant precursor inventories and amino acid production relative to the classic apparatus (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: phosphate-assisted cyanosulfidic network on sulfide mineral surfaces
conditions.other: neutral pH, UV irradiation, Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox chemistry in solution

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde enters phosphoro-Strecker/cyanosulfidic chemistry, giving alpha-aminopropionitrile as the major alanine precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported systems-level cyanosulfidic chemistry producing amino acid precursors, and specifically alpha-aminopropionitrile forms from acetaldehyde in high yield under phosphoro-Strecker conditions at neutral pH with H2S and UV-driven photoredox chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson and Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker addition via ethanimine to alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; acidic or basic pH possible

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to ethanimine, followed by cyanide addition to give alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker mechanism for alanine proceeds through an imine/ethanimine intermediate and then alpha-aminopropionitrile. This route is the most established direct prebiotic pathway from acetaldehyde to alanine precursor (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Stepwise hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide

conditions.other: sequential nitrile hydrolysis under mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile of alpha-aminopropionitrile hydrolyzes first to the corresponding amide, alaninamide.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review specifies sequential hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile through alaninamide before alanine, so this step is separated explicitly to avoid overcompression (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alaninamide to DL-alanine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis in concentrated aqueous pools

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis converts alaninamide to racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential hydrolysis of Strecker aminonitriles to amino acid amides and then amino acids is well established; all such prebiotic syntheses produce racemic alanine rather than enantiopure material (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.other: alkaline to near-neutral vent-associated conditions; oxidative pyrite formation provides reducing equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination with ammonia as FeS is oxidized to pyrite, furnishing alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Direct reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine coupled to pyrite formation was experimentally demonstrated as an iron-sulfur-world analogue of modern biosynthesis (Hafenbradl, Keller, Wachtershauser, and Stetter, 1995).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline conditions consistent with vent-adjacent sediments

reactions.mechanism: Mixed-valence iron minerals mediate electron transfer to convert pyruvate and ammonia into alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed pyruvate-to-alanine conversion driven by mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides under alkaline prebiotic conditions, with maximum yield at 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III), plausibly represented here with reduced iron and magnetite-like mineral redox surfaces (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Ni/H2-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.other: pH 7-8, 30 mM pyruvate, mild prebiotic catalytic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nickel-catalyzed hydrogenation/reductive amination converts pyruvate plus ammonium to alanine under H2.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur, Rauscher, and Werner demonstrated Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine at 22C, pH 7-8, and 5 bar H2, making this one of the most specific modern prebiotic analogues of alanine biosynthesis (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of pyruvate-derived aminonitrile to 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.catalyst: concentrating mineral surfaces in warm evaporitic pools
conditions.other: hydantoin-forming Bucherer-Bergs conditions; CO2 capture by aminonitrile intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia, and CO2 is captured into a cyclic hydantoin framework to form 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Recent work re-emphasized the Bucherer-Bergs-type route in which pyruvate, cyanide, and ammonia lead to 5-methylhydantoin rather than direct nitrile hydrolysis, linking amino acid and nucleobase precursor chemistry (Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen, and Krishnamurthy, 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine

conditions.other: 80C hydrolysis regime

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening of the hydantoin gives the carbamoylated amino acid intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs-derived hydantoin pathway proceeds through 5-methylhydantoin and then N-carbamoyl alanine before alanine itself; this explicit step avoids compressing the literature sequence (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine

conditions.other: continued 80C hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic de-carbamoylation of N-carbamoyl alanine furnishes racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the terminal hydrolysis step of the hydantoin route highlighted by Krishnamurthy and coworkers for prebiotic alanine synthesis (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN under concentrated eutectic or evaporitic conditions

conditions.other: requires HCN concentrations above about 0.01 M; achievable by eutectic freezing or evaporation

reactions.mechanism: HCN self-addition leads through oligomeric nitriles to tetrameric DAMN.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the canonical HCN tetramer and a major gateway intermediate in concentrated HCN chemistry; eutectic concentration is needed to access this regime prebiotically (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa, Cleaves, and Miller, 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer material to alanine-containing product pool

conditions.other: represents hydrolysis of DAMN-derived HCN polymeric material, not a selective single-product conversion

reactions.mechanism: Complex hydrolysis and rearrangement of oligomerized HCN material releases amino acids including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports alanine generation upon hydrolysis of black HCN polymeric material, but not as a clean direct DAMN-to-alanine step. This reaction is therefore intentionally described as a low-specificity product-pool route to avoid the overcompression criticized in previous attempts (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Formose production of glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling and UV-exposed subaerial pools

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde enters formose-type aldol chemistry to generate higher carbonyl compounds including glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Formose chemistry provides higher carbonyl feedstocks from formaldehyde, and borate minerals stabilize reactive sugars and sugar aldehydes in surface settings relevant to prebiotic chemistry (Pinto et al., 1980; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Aldol-based route from sugar aldehydes to acetaldehyde feedstock pool

conditions.catalyst: borate-mediated carbonyl stabilization under UV-exposed cycles
conditions.other: network-level representation of aldehyde interconversion in shallow pools

reactions.mechanism: Carbonyl interconversion within photochemical sugar/aldehyde networks furnishes acetaldehyde as a Strecker substrate.
reactions.reasoning: The deep review identifies multiple prebiotic routes to acetaldehyde and emphasizes aldehyde network interconversion rather than a single exclusive source. This step is included as a necessary but conservative connecting abstraction from known formaldehyde-derived surface carbonyl chemistry, grounded in aldehyde precursor work rather than claimed as a dedicated high-yield alanine step (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide amino-acid synthesis yielding alanine among products

conditions.other: 10-day hydrothermal incubation; complex product mixture

reactions.mechanism: Hydrothermal carbonyl, nitrile, and thio-organic feedstocks react on Fe/Ni sulfide surfaces to produce a suite of amino acids including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wachtershauser reported alpha-amino acid formation, including alanine, in hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide systems from CO-, cyanide-, and thiol-containing feedstocks. This is intentionally represented as a mixture-producing route rather than an over-specific direct single-step alanine synthesis (Huber and Wachtershauser, 2006).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic network-level L-enrichment from DL-alanine pool

conditions.catalyst: none as a single validated chemical catalyst
conditions.other: iterative dissolution-crystallization, compartmentalization, and network amplification; explicitly speculative for alanine

reactions.mechanism: A racemic alanine pool undergoes indirect symmetry breaking and amplification through systems-level coupling to chiral selection processes rather than a single direct enantioselective synthesis step.
reactions.reasoning: The literature review is explicit that no established abiotic pathway directly yields L-alanine; all validated syntheses are racemic. Terrestrial homochirality is instead hypothesized to arise through indirect amplification mechanisms such as CISS-driven chiral selection on magnetite, eutectic/crystallization amplification, and network autocatalysis, potentially coupled to RNA-first selection. This reaction is therefore necessary to satisfy the target specification but is explicitly marked speculative and systems-level rather than an experimentally validated direct alanine-resolving reaction (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Blackmond et al., 2025; Glavin et al., 2025).

pathways.name: Classic Miller-Strecker alanine pathway
pathways.description: Reducing-atmosphere spark discharge generates HCN and acetaldehyde, followed by Strecker synthesis and stepwise hydrolysis to DL-alanine, then late L-enrichment.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S Miller-Strecker pathway
pathways.description: H2S-bearing volcanic spark chemistry enhances alanine precursor formation before standard Strecker hydrolysis chemistry.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to surface aldehyde Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced C1 chemistry supplies formaldehyde, which feeds surface photoredox aldehyde chemistry and then Strecker conversion to alanine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde enters Patel-Sutherland cyanosulfidic chemistry to give alpha-aminopropionitrile efficiently, then hydrolyzes to alanine.

pathways.name: Pyrite-coupled hydrothermal pyruvate amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation generates pyruvate, which undergoes pyrite-driven reductive amination to racemic alanine.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron pyruvate amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is aminated on mixed-valence iron mineral assemblages in alkaline vent-adjacent settings.

pathways.name: Ni-H2 mild reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal or proto-metabolic pyruvate feeds a nickel-catalyzed H2-driven alanine synthesis under mild pH 7-8 conditions.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with ammonia and cyanide to form 5-methylhydantoin, which hydrolyzes through N-carbamoyl alanine to alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis pathway
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and related polymeric material, whose hydrolysis releases alanine among mixed amino acid products.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fe-Ni sulfide complex-mixture alanine pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide chemistry from CO, cyanide, and thiols produces alanine as part of a broader amino-acid product mixture.
